bins.name: Flexible Load Fast Track
bins.desc: AEMA's central proposal is a dedicated Flexible Load Fast Track giving loads that make verifiable flexibility commitments an expedited 60-to-90-day study process. Flexibility must be defined in performance-based, technology-neutral terms covering temporal, spatial, resource, and adjacent flexibility, with bounded upper limits on curtailment obligations so operators have investment certainty.
quotes: AEMA urges the Commission to create a Flexible Load Fast Track (FLFT) for facilities that can verifiably adjust their consumption in response to system conditions and harness a broad set of technologies to deliver flexibility to the grid.

The FLFT would be an expedited (60-90 day) interconnection process for loads that commit to flexibility and would incorporate this flexibility into studies that determine whether transmission grid upgrades are necessary to interconnect the load.

The Commission should direct transmission providers to treat this verifiable reduction as equivalent to a system-impact mitigation, allowing such projects to be studied and interconnected on a shorter timeline.

AEMA recommends that the interconnection study process be used to define clear, upper-bound limits on the required flexibility (e.g., maximum number of hours per year, hours per event, depth of flexibility, and events per year) needed to mitigate specific system constraints.

The final rule should remain strictly technology-neutral, focusing not on the method used, but on the measurable, verifiable, and reliable performance of the resources at their point of interconnection.

Under this model, the operator issues a dispatch signal, for example a 100-megawatt curtailment request to the facility's registered energy management interface. The facility's flexibility management platform executes the reduction automatically and provides real-time or near-real-time, auditable telemetry back to the operator to confirm compliance.
Fast-acting load adjustments can reduce the frequency of local congestion events, defer transmission upgrades, and moderate wholesale price volatility.

AEMA believes the FLFT provides a crucial, market-based choice for large loads: (1) interconnect as an inflexible load and potentially bear the costs for the resulting, often slow and expensive, network upgrades; or (2) interconnect as a flexible load via the FLFT, avoid triggering those upgrades, and get online faster at lower cost. This protects all other ratepayers from socializing upgrade costs.
Coordinate with NERC (Principle 14) to establish uniform telemetry and performance reporting standards, which can enable the 'verifiable' aspect of the FLFT.

bins.name: Expedited Studies with Transparency
bins.desc: AEMA supports standardized interconnection studies that treat verifiable load reductions as system-impact mitigation and provide transparency on specific constraints, curtailment frequency, and amounts. The filing also supports queue opt-in for existing applicants and ISO pilots to accelerate learning.
quotes: AEMA urges the Commission to create a Flexible Load Fast Track (FLFT) for facilities that can verifiably adjust their consumption in response to system conditions and harness a broad set of technologies to deliver flexibility to the grid.

The FLFT would be an expedited (60-90 day) interconnection process for loads that commit to flexibility and would incorporate this flexibility into studies that determine whether transmission grid upgrades are necessary to interconnect the load.

Current state-by-state processes are inconsistent, opaque, and time-consuming, resulting in multi-year delays that slow economic growth amid an AI revolution that presents a critical opportunity for U.S. innovation, competitiveness, and prosperity.

The Commission should direct transmission providers to treat this verifiable reduction as equivalent to a system-impact mitigation, allowing such projects to be studied and interconnected on a shorter timeline.

To comply with Principle 7 of the ANOPR and for large loads to decide whether they can be curtailable, transmission owners and RTOs must provide transparency during the study process regarding the specific constraints that a large load would create and the frequency and amount of curtailment that would be necessary.
AEMA supports a clear transition plan (Principle 13) that allows projects already in the queue to opt in to the FLFT if they meet its flexibility and readiness criteria.

bins.name: Upgrade Cost Allocation via Flexibility
bins.desc: AEMA argues that integrating flexibility into interconnection studies defers or eliminates costly transmission upgrades, keeping costs down for all ratepayers. The FLFT creates a market-based choice: inflexible loads bear upgrade costs while flexible loads avoid them.
quotes: The FLFT would be an expedited (60-90 day) interconnection process for loads that commit to flexibility and would incorporate this flexibility into studies that determine whether transmission grid upgrades are necessary to interconnect the load.
Fast-acting load adjustments can reduce the frequency of local congestion events, defer transmission upgrades, and moderate wholesale price volatility.

AEMA believes the FLFT provides a crucial, market-based choice for large loads: (1) interconnect as an inflexible load and potentially bear the costs for the resulting, often slow and expensive, network upgrades; or (2) interconnect as a flexible load via the FLFT, avoid triggering those upgrades, and get online faster at lower cost. This protects all other ratepayers from socializing upgrade costs.

bins.name: Expedited Process for Curtailable Loads
bins.desc: AEMA endorses an expedited interconnection path for loads committing to curtailability, aligned with the ANOPR's Principle 7 and the SPP High Impact Large Load precedent. Study transparency on constraints is a prerequisite for loads to make informed curtailability commitments.
quotes: AEMA urges the Commission to create a Flexible Load Fast Track (FLFT) for facilities that can verifiably adjust their consumption in response to system conditions and harness a broad set of technologies to deliver flexibility to the grid.

The FLFT would be an expedited (60-90 day) interconnection process for loads that commit to flexibility and would incorporate this flexibility into studies that determine whether transmission grid upgrades are necessary to interconnect the load.

The Commission should direct transmission providers to treat this verifiable reduction as equivalent to a system-impact mitigation, allowing such projects to be studied and interconnected on a shorter timeline.

To comply with Principle 7 of the ANOPR and for large loads to decide whether they can be curtailable, transmission owners and RTOs must provide transparency during the study process regarding the specific constraints that a large load would create and the frequency and amount of curtailment that would be necessary.

bins.name: Operator Interface as System Protection
bins.desc: AEMA proposes a secure, standardized operator-facility interface with automated dispatch and auditable telemetry as the modern system protection mechanism, mirroring demand-response verification practices while preserving cybersecurity and operational autonomy.
quotes: Under this model, the operator issues a dispatch signal, for example a 100-megawatt curtailment request to the facility's registered energy management interface. The facility's flexibility management platform executes the reduction automatically and provides real-time or near-real-time, auditable telemetry back to the operator to confirm compliance.

bins.name: Non-Firm Withdrawal Rights for Curtailable Portion
bins.desc: AEMA recommends a new tariff product giving FLFT facilities non-firm withdrawal rights for their curtailable portion, allowing them to combine firm service for mission-critical operations with separately curtailable capacity and corresponding ancillary service obligations.
quotes: A 'Non-Firm Withdrawal Right,' for example, could be an appropriate tariff-based underpinning. This clarification ensures facilities can combine firm service for mission-critical loads with a separate, curtailable portion, which would in turn have different ancillary service responsibilities.

bins.name: Readiness Requirements Scaled to Large Loads
bins.desc: AEMA supports standardized readiness criteria such as deposits and site control for the FLFT, consistent with generator interconnection but appropriately scaled for large loads, to deter speculative projects while enabling the cost-allocation benefits of the flexible track.
quotes: AEMA believes the FLFT provides a crucial, market-based choice for large loads: (1) interconnect as an inflexible load and potentially bear the costs for the resulting, often slow and expensive, network upgrades; or (2) interconnect as a flexible load via the FLFT, avoid triggering those upgrades, and get online faster at lower cost. This protects all other ratepayers from socializing upgrade costs.

bins.name: Uniform National Process
bins.desc: AEMA criticizes inconsistent, opaque state-by-state interconnection processes that cause multi-year delays and urges FERC to impose a standardized national framework. It also supports a transition plan letting queued projects opt into the FLFT.
quotes: Current state-by-state processes are inconsistent, opaque, and time-consuming, resulting in multi-year delays that slow economic growth amid an AI revolution that presents a critical opportunity for U.S. innovation, competitiveness, and prosperity.
AEMA supports a clear transition plan (Principle 13) that allows projects already in the queue to opt in to the FLFT if they meet its flexibility and readiness criteria.

bins.name: Four-Category Flexibility Definition
bins.desc: AEMA recommends defining flexibility across four functional categories (temporal, spatial, resource, and adjacent) with bounded obligations and a technology-neutral, performance-based focus on measurable outcomes at the point of interconnection.
quotes: AEMA recommends that the interconnection study process be used to define clear, upper-bound limits on the required flexibility (e.g., maximum number of hours per year, hours per event, depth of flexibility, and events per year) needed to mitigate specific system constraints.

The final rule should remain strictly technology-neutral, focusing not on the method used, but on the measurable, verifiable, and reliable performance of the resources at their point of interconnection.

bins.name: Study Transparency on Constraints
bins.desc: AEMA urges transmission owners and RTOs to disclose specific grid constraints and curtailment obligations during the study process so loads can make informed decisions about entering the FLFT.
quotes: To comply with Principle 7 of the ANOPR and for large loads to decide whether they can be curtailable, transmission owners and RTOs must provide transparency during the study process regarding the specific constraints that a large load would create and the frequency and amount of curtailment that would be necessary.

bins.name: Standardized Operator-Facility Interface
bins.desc: AEMA advocates for a secure, standardized software or hardware interface through which operators issue dispatch signals and receive auditable telemetry confirming compliance, with NERC coordination to establish uniform standards across regions.
quotes: Under this model, the operator issues a dispatch signal, for example a 100-megawatt curtailment request to the facility's registered energy management interface. The facility's flexibility management platform executes the reduction automatically and provides real-time or near-real-time, auditable telemetry back to the operator to confirm compliance.
Coordinate with NERC (Principle 14) to establish uniform telemetry and performance reporting standards, which can enable the 'verifiable' aspect of the FLFT.

bins.name: New Tariff Product for FLFT
bins.desc: AEMA recommends a new non-firm withdrawal right tariff product to underpin the FLFT, allowing flexible loads to hold firm service for critical operations and non-firm service for their curtailable capacity with appropriate ancillary service obligations.
quotes: A 'Non-Firm Withdrawal Right,' for example, could be an appropriate tariff-based underpinning. This clarification ensures facilities can combine firm service for mission-critical loads with a separate, curtailable portion, which would in turn have different ancillary service responsibilities.
